title: Paper Title
content: Photoreduction of CO2 using copper-decorated TiO2 nanorod films with localized surface plasmon behavior

title: Abstract
content: This Letter shows the activity of a novel metalsemiconductor photocatalyst for reducing CO 2 to CH 4 in the presence of H 2 O under UV-light irradiation.This composite is prepared on a transparent conductive substrate by an hydrothermal method for depositing TiO 2 nanorod films, and then loaded with Cu nanoparticles (NPs) by an electrochemical method.The plasmonic properties of Cu NPs greatly enhance the resulting photoactivity with respect to that of pure TiO 2 nanorod films.The final CH 4 production rate observed (2.91 ppmg catal.h) represents an improvement compared to specific values reported by other authors using conventional titania-based catalysts.

title: Experimental
content: The growth of oriented TiO 2 nanorods was carried using a simple hydrothermal method following the literature 8.Fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) pieces (F:SnO 2 , Tec 15, 10 Xm, Hartford Glass Company) which were ultrasonically cleaned for 60 min in a mixed solution of deionized water, acetone and 2-propanol with volume ratios of 1:1:1, were employed as substrate for the growth of nanorods.In a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (500 mL volume, Jinan Henghua Sci. Tec.Co. Ltd.), deionized water and concentrated hydrochloric acid (36.5-38 by weight) were mixed (ratio 1:1) to reach a total volume of 300 mL, and stirred at ambient conditions for 5 min before the addition of 6 mL of titanium butoxide (97 Aldrich).After stirring for another 5 min, 180 drops of surfactant polyethylene imine (PEI, Chine Midwest Group, Beijing) with a molecular weight of 5000 were added to the solution, and FTO substrates were placed at an angle against the wall of the Teflon-liner with the conducting side facing down.The hydrothermal synthesis was then conducted at 120 C for 20 h.After synthesis had been achieved, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature.The FTO substrate was removed, rinsed extensively with deionized water overnight and allowed to dry in ambient conditions.The deposition of Cu nanoparticles onto the thin TiO 2 films obtained was performed via an electrochemical method using an electrochemical workstation instrument (ES550, Gaoss Union Technology Co., Ltd., Wuhan, China).Cu solution was prepared from 0.01 mM of CuSO 4 and 0.1 M of K 2 SO 4 .Based on a previously scanned solution for the electrode potential of clean FTO, a pulse potential of A0.8 V was chosen to deposit Cu atoms at deposition times ranging from 0 to 300 s. The morphologies of TiO 2 nanorods and the distribution of Cu nanoparticles were examined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4800).The crystalline phase of the TiO 2 thin films was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a X'Pert PRO diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation (1.5406 A) from 5to 65at a scanning speed of 2min.The surface composition was evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a VG ESCALAB Mark II instrument using a Mg-Ka excitation source.Spectra were recorded at normal emission with pass energies of 0.8 eV at 300 W, and the amount of Cu nanoparticles on TiO 2 films was calculated from the area under the curve according to the Handbook of X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Physical Electronics Division, Eden Prairie, Minnesota, USA, 1979).Nitrogen adsorptiondesorption measurements were conducted on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020M analyzer.The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was calculated using the adsorption data.UV-visible absorption spectra were obtained on an UV-visible spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Purkinje General Instrument CO., Ltd., Beijing, China).The photoluminescence spectra were measured at 298 K with a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburg Instruments Ltd.) using a 325 nm UV xenon lamp as the excitation source. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was conducted in a flat bottom-stainless steel reactor (capacity 200 ml) with a quartz window on the top.One gram of the prepared TiO 2 films was placed at the center of the reactor and deionized water was added until the film was completely covered.The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated under ultraviolet-light irradiation of four 8 W UVA lamps with a wavelength of 365 nm (average intensity: 3.25 mW cm 2 , measured by UVX radiometer, UVP), located in two groups on opposing sides of the reactor.Before illumination, ultra-pure gaseous CO 2 (Air Products, 99.995) was bubble through the reactor for 30 min to degas the water.After purging, the reactor was pressurized with gaseous CO 2 at 1 bar, and held for another 30 min to saturate the water with CO 2 .Irradiation was periodically stopped to permit gas and liquid sampling from the reactor for subsequent analysis by gas chromatography (GCFID, Thermo-Fisher, Trace GC) during the 72 h total reaction time.Methane was the only organic product resulting from CO 2 photoreduction within the detection limits of our method (200 ppb).Methane yield was thus adopted as a direct measure of activity towards CO 2 photocatalytic reduction.For comparison, a similar photocatalytic experiment was conducted with commercial anatase titania (Across Organics, 99).

title: Results and discussion
content: The surface morphology of TiO 2 nanorod films before and after Cu incorporation using various deposition times was observed by scanning electron microscopy.Figure 1 presents the top (a) and cross-sectional (b-e) views of the prepared films, which reveal that the entire surface of the FTO substrate is evenly covered with TiO 2 nanorods.The nanorods are tetragonal in shape with the top facets being square, lying almost perpendicular to the FTO substrate, and with lengths of approximately 1 lm.After electrodeposition, only the sample with a copper loading ratio corresponding to 100 s of deposition time (Figure 1c) presents a uniform dispersion of the metal on the surface of the support, as well as maintaining integrity of the titania nanorods.Increasing deposition times (i.e.greater Cu loading) give: (i) the appearance of milky-colored spots on the TiO 2 nanorods, probably as a result of the agglomeration (van der Waals interactions) or aggregation (chemical bonds) of Cu NPs, occurring mainly at the bottom part of nanorods (Figure 1d), or even (ii) the sintering damage and fracture of the structure of the titania nanorods (Figure 1e). XRD patterns of the prepared TiO 2 nanorods (Figure 2) also confirmed that the films deposited on FTO substrates are rutile TiO 2 with growth axis along the (1 0 1) and (0 0 2) directions.Furthermore, no peaks attributed to the loaded copper can be found in the XRD patterns even for the titania catalysts with the highest metal loading ratio, i.e. after 300 s of deposition time.This could be due to the relatively low concentration of the loaded metal, as reported before by other authors 31.Figure 2a andb represent XRD patterns of pure titania nanorods before and after reaction, showing that the photoreduction process did not modify the crystallinity of titania films from their initial stage.However, the copper incorporation was found to affect the orientation of TiO 2 nanorods.While XRD patterns collected from pure TiO 2 nanorods show a strongly preferential orientation of (0 0 2), the most intense reflection for Cu-TiO 2 nanorod films (Figure 2c-e) was (1 0 1). UV-vis absorption measurements were carried out over the prepared titania nanorods films (see Figure 3).The presence of copper NPs on titania nanorods improves the absorbance with their optical absorption spectra dominated by two LSPR peaks ranging between 400-420 nm and 575-590 nm, the former being of greatest importance.On carefully observing the LSPR curves we notice a red shift and a broadening in both peaks with the increase of the deposition time.From SEM observations it is clear that the sample produced after 100 s of deposition time presents the greatest metal dispersion, suggesting a smaller NP size.Therefore the aggregation of Cu NPs may be causing a red-shift in the absorbance readings, while the smaller and more compact nanostructures lead to a blue shift.This is because the position and width of the LSPR band depends on the density of free electrons in the Cu NPs and an increase in e A density of the smaller NPs results in a blue shift of LSPR 23.The broader absorption bands have also been suggested as indication of larger particle size 32. The photoluminescence of pure TiO 2 and Cu-TiO 2 nanorod films is shown in Figure 4.The wavelength of fluorescence was in the range of 400-500 nm under UV excitation at around 325 nm.The emitted fluorescence of Cu-TiO 2 films was in the same region as that of pure TiO 2 film, but at much low intensity.The existence of photoluminescence can be attributed to the recombination of excited electron-hole pairs 33.Accordingly, the decline in the intensity of fluorescence reveals the decrease in the rate of recombination of electron-hole pairs on metal-loaded TiO 2 nanorod films.It is believed that a charge transfer mechanism 34 between titania films and Cu NPs occurs, whereupon the positively-charged plasmons of metal NPs attract electrons in the conduction band of TiO 2 and thus an increased capability of electron-hole separation is observed compared to the pure sample.The suppression of the fluorescence continues to increase as the deposition time of Cu NPs increases, suggesting that both the morphology of Cu NPs and their geometric placement with respect to each other play an important role in the charge recombination dynamics involved in this system.It has been previously reported 34 that the LSPRinduced electromagnetic fields in the vicinity of the plasmonic NPs depend on their composition, shape, size and dielectric environment.Therefore, different rates of e A h formation and recombination in the semiconductor, as well as re-dox reactions may be expected 34 when one considers different plasmonic blocks.Smitha et al. 32 observed that the photoemission wavelength is independent of the particle size while the intensity increases sharply with decrease of particle size.In the case of larger aggregates (e.g.Cu NPs produced after 300 s of deposition time) the temperance in both electromagnetic and electric fields of the plasmons has also been observed to give rise to the reduction of electron-hole pair separation in the semiconductor 35. The photoreduction of CO 2 was used to further evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the TiO 2 nanorod film with a Cu load corresponding to 100 s of deposition time.Degussa P25 TiO 2 and pure TiO 2 nanorod film without Cu impregnation were chosen as the reference photocatalysts for comparison.The photocatalytic results are shown in Figure 5, represented as methane yield (a) and methane production rate (b) versus reaction time.Both pure and metal-deposited TiO 2 nanorod films exhibited stronger methane yields, expressed as cumulative amount of CH 4 released, than P25.In this last case, the CH 4 yield remains steady after 24 h, which indicates that not more CH 4 is being produced as a result of the deactivation of TiO 2 P25, which is also reflected by the decrease in the methane production rate.The structural, morphological and optical characteristics of TiO 2 nanorod films are key factors in the explanation of their greater photoactivity during CO 2 conversion to CH 4 .On one hand, XRD patterns of the TiO 2 nanorods (see Figure 2) showed pure (1 0 1) rutile phase, which is the active reduction site ascribed to surface electron traps 34.However P25 is a mixture of 80 anatase and 20 rutile phases attributed to a larger extent to surface hole traps, in such a way that photooxidation reactions are more preferable than those of photoreduction.Therefore, when the photoreduction sites on P25 finally reach their saturation, the conversion yield remains at the same level.However, the greater availability of reactive sites in the rutile (1 0 1) nanorods enables more CO 2 adsorption 36, so conversion yield increases as time proceeds.A high surface area is also critically important to increase the number of red-ox reaction sites.In this sense TiO 2 nanorod films exhibit twice the surface area in comparison to P25 (117 m 2 g versus 50 m 2 g, respectively), which stand in very good agreement with experimental results presented in Figure 5.Meanwhile, the nanorod morphology also provides an increased diffusion length of photoinduced electrons and thus maintaining a superior lifetime of electron-hole pairs for transference to adsorbed reactants 16.Greater light absorption also improves the photocatalytic activity of TiO 2 nanorod films, and therefore, increased CO 2 conversion is obtained in comparison to P25. Interestingly, the addition of Cu NPs to the TiO 2 nanorod films greatly enhances photocatalytic efficiency and may even quadruple the final methane yield observed (200 ppmg catal.) upon completion of the experiments compared to using unmodified TiO 2 films (50 ppmg catal.).The methane production rates are also consistent with these results, since the Cu-TiO 2 nanorod films show about twice the rate observed with pure titania films.While the former exhibit a constant increase during the time frame for which the experiments were conducted, the latter keep on the same yield rate of CH 4 , which points out the substantial role played by Cu.It is believed that the deposition of Cu NPs assists in the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes from recombination in TiO 2 but with only mild LSPR effects.This is because copper is easily being oxidized and is unstable in air 37, as revealed by the presence of copper oxides in the XPS analyses (see Figure 6).In this sense, the coating of plasmonic NPs with a SiO 2 shell has been suggested by other authors 32 to prevent foremost their oxidation.Meanwhile, the loading of Cu NPs on the active (1 0 1) rutile phase of TiO 2 also provides more surface electron traps than pure TiO 2 nanorods 38.The homogeneous distribution of Cu NPs over the entire nanorod array surface area is also a factor responsible for superior catalytic performances, as it may further improve utilization of the intermediates carrying out photocatalytic reductions at enhanced rates 16.The results obtained show an improvement in term of the CH 4 yield compared to values reported by other authors 39,40 using metallic doped-titania catalysts in powder form. XPS studies were conducted over the selected Cu-doped TiO 2 film (after 100 s of deposition time) to further investigate the promotion of photoreduction as a result of copper loading, and to explore which chemical state of Cu is the main contributor to the enhancement of TiO 2 activity in this Letter.Figure 6 displays the XPS spectra of Cu (2p) indicating that the copper on the surface of TiO 2 nanorod films exhibits multiple oxidation estates.A comparison of the spectra of CuO (binding energies of 933.8 and 953.8 eV for the Cu2p 32 and Cu2p 12 peaks, respectively) with that of Cu 2 O (binding energies of 932.8 and 952.8 eV for the Cu2p 32 and Cu2p 12 peaks, respectively) indicates the presence of both Cu(II) and Cu(I) species, although the former dominates as indicated by the greater intensities of the corresponding XPS peaks.Some-stud-ies 22 have reported Cu(I) to be the active state for photoreduction of CO 2 due to its stronger capability of electron reception.Therefore, we assume that reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(I) takes place during the experiments, which would explain the constant increase of methane yield and methane production rate.The transformation of Cu components (Cu mechanistic cycle) in titaniabased photocatalytic systems have also been considered by other authors 41.

title: Conclusions
content: To summarize, we propose a new type of photocatalyst for the photoreduction of CO 2 to CH 4 with yield rates up to 2.91 ppmg ca- tal.h.This novel photocatalyst is based on the combination of TiO 2 nanorod films (providing large adsorption capacity of CO 2 and diffusion length of photoinduced electrons) and copper NPs with LSPR effect.It is conceived that the deposition of plasmonic Cu NPs assists in the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, whereupon the positively-charged plasmons of metal NPs attract electrons in the conduction band of TiO 2 .The optical properties of Cu NPs bear a close relationship to the deposition time used during the electro-deposition process reported here.Studies on the specific role of the morphology and chemical state, as well as their associated LSPR behavior during the CO 2 photomethanation are now under consideration. Figure 1.SEM observations.(a) Top view of pure TiO 2 nanorod film.(b) Cross-sectional view of pure TiO 2 nanorod film.(c) Cross-sectional view of TiO 2 nanorod film after Cu electrodeposition at 100 s ; (d) 200 s and (e) 300 s of deposition time. Figure 3. UV-vis spectra of pure and Cu-TiO 2 nanorod films at different deposition times. Figure 4. Fluorescence spectra of pure and Cu-TiO 2 nanorod films at different deposition times. Figure 5. Evolution of methane yield (a) and methane production rate (b) with time for Degusa P25 TiO 2 , pure and Cu-TiO 2 nanorod films after 100 s of deposition time under UV irradiation (365 nm). Figure 2. XRD patterns of pure TiO 2 nanorod films after (a) and before (b) CO 2 photoreduction, and of TiO 2 nanorod film after Cu electrodeposition at 100 s (c), 200 s (d) and 300 s (e) before reaction. The peaks corresponding to the fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) used as transparent conductive substrate. Figure 6.Cu (2p) XPS spectrum of Cu-TiO 2 nanorodfilms after 100 s of deposition time. J.Z.Y.Tan et al. Chemical Physics Letters 531 (2012) 149-154 This work was financially supported by University of Nottingham -Zhejiang Collaborative Fund, 2011, 'Development of porous TiO 2 films for carbon dioxide conversion into chemicals and fuels'.We are grateful to the support received by M. Maroto-Valer from the Leverhulme Trust (Philip Leverhulme Prize).Y. Fernandez is also grateful to CSIC and FICYT of Spain for financial support under the Program 'Clarin' 2011-2012.
